content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660           322-5901           322-6441           322-6444

May 10, 1978


Mr. R. W. Johnson, P.E.
Director, San Lorenzo Valley
County Water District
Post Office Box H
Boulder Creek, California  95006

Dear Mr. Johnson:

     Thank you for your letter requesting advice with
respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act.  Specifically, you ask whether a
director of a county water district may vote on matters
which may have an effect on the election of his spouse
to a paid position as a county supervisor, during the
time he or she is a candidate.

     Section 87100 of the Government Code provides:

     No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.

     Section 87103 defines financial interest to include:

     ... (c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public official
within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; ....

     Section 82030(b)(2) specifically excludes the
following from the definition of income:

     ... Salary and reimbursement for expenses or
per diem received from a state or local govern-
ment agency and reimbursement for travel
expenses and per diem received from a bona
fide educational, academic or charitable
organization; ....

R. W. Johnson
May 10, 1978                                                    Page Two


     If Ms. Gee is elected to the Board of Supervisors,
she will receive a salary from the County. Since the
County is a local government agency within the meaning
of Government Code Section 82041, these payments are not
"income" within the meaning of Government Code Section
87103. Therefore, even if we assume that the scenario
set forth in your letter does in fact occur, it would
not create a conflict of interest within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act.

     With respect to your comments regarding Mr. Amrhein's
advice, you have not provided us with sufficient facts for
us to determine whether we agree with the advice. However,
I have spoken with Mr. Amrhein on several occasions regard-
ing the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act, and believe him to have a generally good under-
standing of the Act. If either Mr. Amrhein or members of
the Board have special questions, I would be happy to assist
in resolving them.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division


TP:gb


cc:  John Gee

SAN LORENZO VALLEY COUNTY WATER DISTRICT
13060 CENTRAL AVENUE                                 POST OFFICE BOX H
(408) 338-2153                                       BOULDER CREEK, CA 95006

                         April 25, 1978

Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807, 1100 K Street Bldg.
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Bennett:

I am writing you as an individual director of the San Lorenzo
Valley County Water District to inquire about a possible
conflict of interest situation with another one of our directors.

In your opinion request No. 77-023 of October 7, 1977 concerning
director Peterson you set forth answers to specific questions
raised by the district's attorney, Mr. Amrhein, most of which
concerned votes on the district's water connection moratorium.
While I believe the basic foundation logic of this document to
be faulty in that it is demonstrable that the moratorium has
increased property values and that its repeal would decrease, not
increase them, we as a board are attempting to live with the
ruling pending other legal action testing the constitutionality
of your commission.

Mr. Amrhein, however, has been stretching your opinion well beyond
its elastic limit. For example, he has advised us that contrary
to the statement in paragraph 3 that Mr. Peterson may participate
concerning new facilities once the decision is made (within him)
to go ahead with them, Mr. Peterson is forever disqualified from
voting even on relatively minor matters concerning new facilities
which we have already approved. Mr. Amrhein has also taken the
view that Mr. Peterson may not vote on the simple location of a
fire hydrant, or to grant renewal of a prior service, or to waive
a connection fee in a specific case.

My reason for mentioning this is that if it is indeed the intent of
your ruling in 77-023 that we have to reach this far to disqualify
a director from voting, then logically we should also disqualify
another director who has a much more clear-cut conflict at this time.

I am referring to Director John Gee. Your office recently replied to
him that his wife's lawsuit against the district would not produce a
conflict when he assumed his seat on the board. But now, Mrs. Gee has
filed, as of April 7, 1978, as a candidate for county supervisor in
a recall election in the fifth supervisorial district, in which the
water district is entirely situated. This is a paid position, which

under community property laws would result in income to Mr. Gee if
Mrs. Gee were to be elected.

Mr. Gee's votes on the water district board will in many cases be
influential as to the outcome of Mrs. Gee's candidacy. Mrs. Gee is
soliciting support of the same people who voted for Mr. Gee, and
it is clear that Mr. Gee would not want to alienate these people
until after the supervisorial election which is scheduled for
June 6, 1978. Rather, it would be expected that Mr. Gee would go
out of his way to please these supporters even if it meant subordinating
the best interests of the water district.

An example of this is Mr. Gee's recent vote to allow the re-establishment
of two water connections in which it was proved that there once was
a service under the predecessor to this water district. The vote
on these two matters was 5-0, both Mr. Peterson and Mr. Gee voting in
the affirmative.

Apparently responding to pressures from his supporters (many of whom
also wrote nearly identical letters protesting Mr. Peterson's vote
on the matters) Mr. Gee demanded reconsideration of the matters
and changed his position.

I would therefore like your opinion on the following question:

     May a director of a County Water District vote on matters
     which may have an effect on the election of his candidate
     wife to a paid position as a county supervisor, during
     the time she is a candidate?

I would appreciate an early reply, since we have much important business
before us between now and June 6, after which the question will be moot.

Yours very truly,

[signature]

cc:                           R. W. Johnson, P. E.
SLVCWD                        Director, SLVCWD

sections.question: May a director of a County Water District vote on matters which may have an effect on the election of his candidate wife to a paid position as a county supervisor, during the time she is a candidate?
sections.conclusion: If Ms. Gee is elected to the Board of Supervisors, she will receive a salary from the County. Since the County is a local government agency within the meaning of Government Code Section 82041, these payments are not "income" within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103. Therefore, even if we assume that the scenario set forth in your letter does in fact occur, it would not create a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a water district director may vote on matters that could affect his spouse's election to a paid county supervisor position while she is a candidate, given potential community property income implications.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The salary that a spouse would receive as a county supervisor is excluded from the definition of 'income' under the Political Reform Act because it comes from a local government agency, and therefore does not create a disqualifying financial interest under Section 87100.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (April 25, 1978 from R.W. Johnson) and FPPC response letter (May 10, 1978 from Ted Prim). The CONCLUSION section is clearly marked and verbatim text extracted. Question is directly quoted from the incoming request. Response addresses the specific legal question about community property income from elected position.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a director of a County Water District vote on matters which may have an effect on the election of his candidate wife to a paid position as a county supervisor, during the time she is a candidate?

CONCLUSION: If Ms. Gee is elected to the Board of Supervisors, she will receive a salary from the County. Since the County is a local government agency within the meaning of Government Code Section 82041, these payments are not "income" within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103. Therefore, even if we assume that the scenario set forth in your letter does in fact occur, it would not create a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.

Sincerely, Ted Prim, Chief Conflicts of Interest Division TP:gb cc: John Gee SAN LORENZO VALLEY COUNTY WATER DISTRICT 13060 CENTRAL AVENUE POST OFFICE BOX H (408) 338-2153 BOULDER CREEK, CA 95006 April 25, 1978 Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a water district director's inquiry regarding whether another director has a conflict of interest in voting on matters while his wife is a candidate for county supervisor. The FPPC concludes that the wife's potential county supervisor salary is not 'income' under the Political Reform Act and therefore creates no conflict of interest.
